title: NOC 80022 – Managers in aquaculture | GTR Immigration
description: Managers in aquaculture under NOC 80022 are the specialized leaders driving Canada's rapidly expanding blue economy. As a TEER 0 (Management) occupation, these professionals oversee the cultivation and harvesting of fish, shellfish, and marine plants in hatcheries and sea farms. In 2026, with global demand for sustainable protein at record highs, aquaculture managers are critical for implementing high-tech biosecurity, automated feeding systems, and environmental monitoring. For immigration, this management role is a top-tier candidate for the Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP) and the new 2026 Sector-Specific Stream for Agriculture and Fish Processing.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category8 – Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupationsTEER Level0 (Management)Major Group80 – Management occupations in natural resources, agriculture and related productionSub-Major Group800 – Management occupations in natural resources, agriculture and related productionMinor Group8002 – Managers in agriculture, horticulture and aquacultureNOC Code80022

duties: Manage the overall operation of a fish hatchery, fish farm, or other aquatic farm facility.
Identify requirements of specific species and oversee the preparation of sites for cultivation.
Coordinate the selection and maintenance of broodstock and determine optimal feeding regimes.
Monitor environment and water quality to maintain optimum conditions for species health.
Supervise stock examinations to identify diseases or parasites and apply prescribed treatments.
Collect and record growth, production, and environmental data for regulatory reporting.
Manage and train support workers, technicians, and technologists.
Establish market strategies, manage financial records, and maintain quality control methods.
employment_requirements: Education: A college diploma in aquaculture or a related field (e.g., Marine Biology) is usually required.
Experience: Several years of experience in fishing or aquaculture operations are typically mandatory for management roles.
Licensing: A commercial aquaculture or fish hatchery license, permit, or lease is required for self-employed managers.
Specialized Skills: Proficiency in Scuba Diving may be required for inspecting sea farm operations (cages, collector strings).
salary_prospects: Note: High-level directors and managers of large-scale commercial salmon operations can earn annual salaries exceeding $150,000.
job_outlook: The outlook is Favourable. Global seafood demand is projected to grow by 9% through 2027, with aquaculture meeting a large share of this need. As Canada transitions toward land-based and offshore systems to protect wild habitats, the complexity of operations will increase, driving the need for professional managers with strong technical and administrative backgrounds.
labour_market_demand: The demand for aquaculture managers is Good, particularly in coastal regions. In 2026, British Columbia remains the largest hub for salmon and shellfish farming, while Nova Scotia, New Brunswick, and Prince Edward Island are seeing increased demand for managers to lead oyster, mussel, and land-based recirculating aquaculture systems (RAS).
Leading the Blue Economy: Manage operations in a country with the world's longest coastline and a global reputation for sustainable seafood.
Technological Integration: Lead the use of underwater drones, AI-driven stock health sensors, and advanced water filtration systems.
Environmental Stewardship: Play a direct role in wild stock enhancement and the development of "carbon-sink" seaweed farming projects.
PR Strategic Advantage: As a TEER 0 management role, this occupation significantly boosts CRS scores in Express Entry and is a priority for Atlantic provincial nominations.
pathways: Express Entry (Federal Skilled Worker): High CRS points for management-level experience.
New 2026 Agriculture and Fish Processing Stream: A dedicated pathway designed specifically to address labor needs in the fish cultivation sector.
Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): A high-priority route for those with a job offer in the Atlantic provinces.
BC PNP Skills Immigration: Targeted draws for the natural resources and agriculture sectors.
